elements.sent-2411-001: This paper introduces a novel approach to efficient localization in next-generation communication systems through a base station (BS)-enabled passive beamforming utilizing beyond diagonal reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (BD-RISs).
elements.sent-2411-002: Unlike conventional diagonal RISs (D-RISs), which suffer from limited beamforming capability, a BD-RIS provides enhanced control over both phase and amplitude, significantly improving localization accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-003: By conducting a comprehensive Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) analysis across various system parameters in both near-field and far-field scenarios, we establish the BD-RIS structure as a competitive alternative to traditional active antenna arrays.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our results reveal that BD-RISs achieve near active antenna arrays performance in localization precision, overcoming the limitations of D-RISs and underscoring its potential for high-accuracy positioning in future communication networks.
elements.sent-2411-005: This work envisions the use of BD-RIS for enabling passive beamforming-based localization, setting the stage for more efficient and scalable localization strategies in sixth-generation networks and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-006: In sixth-generation (6G) communication systems, high-precision localization is expected to be achieved with a single anchor, enabled by advances in millimeter wave (mmWave) communication and large antenna arrays \cite{9129075, 9145059 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Such technologies allow base stations (BSs) or user equipments (UEs) to measure angles of departure (AoD) and arrival (AoA) more precisely.
elements.sent-2411-008: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) offer a promising, cost-effective alternative to large antenna arrays by enabling passive beamforming within transceivers \cite{10515204 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: For instance, the stacked intelligent metasurface (SIM) transceiver design \cite{10158690, 10515204, 10679332 , 10643881 demonstrates the potential of passive beamforming with benefits such as ultra-fast processing, reduced cost and complexity, and lower energy consumption.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Beyond diagonal RIS (BD-RIS) advances RIS technology by enabling control over both the amplitude and phase of impinging waves, offering higher flexibility in passive beamforming \cite{li2023reconfigurable, 9514409 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Notably, unlike active or absorptive RIS \cite{10251110 , BD-RIS controls both phase and amplitude of the impinging signal under a unitary constraint, without amplification or absorption.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The fundamental limits of mmWave multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems with large RISs, focusing on channel estimation, localization, and orientation error bounds, are studied in \cite{9129075 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The study of \cite{10308579 investigates the use of a BD-RIS at the transmitter for massive MIMO, using manifold optimization to enhance spectral efficiency with fewer active antennas.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Similarly, \cite{chen2024transmitter shows that BD-RIS enhances both communication and sensing performance in mmWave integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, significantly reducing power consumption and presenting a promising solution for future applications.}
elements.sent-2411-015: RIS-aided near-field (NF) localization is further explored in \cite{10017173, 10436860 , showing the potential for NF localization by exploiting spherical wavefront.}
elements.sent-2411-016: A SIM-aided ISAC system, with shared elements for communication and sensing, is proposed in \cite{10643881 , while the role of SIM for direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation is examined in \cite{10622963, 10557708 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In scenarios such as those studied in \cite{10017173, 10643881, 10622963, 10557708 , where part or all of the channel operates in the NF, the signal undergoes amplitude variations across different RIS elements alongside phase changes.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Diagonal RIS (D-RIS), however, only enables phase adjustment, whereas BD-RIS allows control over both amplitude and phase.
elements.sent-2411-019: Despite this added flexibility, the localization potential of BD-RIS remains unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-020: This gap in the literature motivates our investigation of BD-RIS’s potential for precision localization in such scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-021: The main contributions of this paper are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-022: We propose a BS-enabled passive beamforming system using a BD-RIS for downlink efficient localization, a scenario that has not been previously explored.
elements.sent-2411-023: We analyze the beamforming gain of the proposed system, demonstrating the superiority of BD-RISs over traditional structures.
elements.sent-2411-024: Additionally, Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) analysis in both NF and far-field (FF) scenarios is performed.
elements.sent-2411-025: We conduct a comprehensive analysis, evaluating localization performance across various system parameters.
elements.sent-2411-026: This detailed analysis offers new insights into the potential of BD-RIS, highlighting its capability to enhance precision localization from multiple perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-027: The notations $(.)^\mathsf{H}$, $(.)^\mathsf{T}$, $||.||$, $\diag(.)$, and $\angle$ signify the Hermitian, transpose, norm, diagonalization, and phase of a complex number.
elements.sent-2411-028: The derivative of $\boldsymbol{b}$ with respect to $q$ is denoted as $\dot{\boldsymbol{b _q = \partial \boldsymbol{b / \partial q$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The notation $[.]_\ell$ indicates the $\ell$-th element of a vector, while $[.]_{\ell,s}$ specifies the entry in the $\ell$-th row and $s$-th column of a matrix.
elements.sent-2411-030: Furthermore, $[a:\Delta:b]$ represents a discrete sequence from $a$ to $b$ in steps of $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-031: This paper analyzes two scenarios: Scenario 1 focuses on the NF case, while Scenario 2 addresses the FF case.
elements.sent-2411-032: To maintain consistency and simplify notation, any quantity or variable specific to Scenario $i \in \{ 1, 2 \}$ is indexed with the subscript $i$ throughout this paper.
elements.sent-2411-033: This section outlines the proposed system, channel, and signal model, which leverages BS-enabled passive beamforming assisted by a BD-RIS to facilitate downlink localization.
elements.sent-2411-034: Fig.~\ref{fig:System Model illustrates the proposed system designed to enable passive beamforming at the BS in a single-input single-output (SISO) communication system, where a linear fully-connected BD-RIS is integrated with the BS to emulate a multiple-input single-output (MISO) system.\footnote{This work is the first to explore the integration of fully-connected BD-RIS at the BS for passive beamforming in localization.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We focus on 2D localization to simplify the analysis and provide a clear understanding of the system’s behavior, reserving the more complex 3D analysis for future research.
elements.sent-2411-036: The BD-RIS consists of $M$ cells, each containing two elements: one faces the active antenna, while the other on the opposite side transmits signals toward the UE \cite{9913356 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The inter-element spacing on both sides of the BD-RIS is $\delta = \lambda/2$, where $\lambda$ denotes the wavelength of the carrier frequency.
elements.sent-2411-038: Accordingly, the array aperture of BD-RIS can be calculated as $D = (M-1)\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-039: All elements are internally connected, forming a fully-connected BD-RIS structure \cite{li2023reconfigurable .}
elements.sent-2411-040: For passive beamforming to occur at the BS, the total energy of the signal emitted by the active antenna must pass through the BD-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-041: As a result, the BD-RIS operates exclusively in transmissive mode, with its reflective mode inactive \cite{9913356 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: For simplicity, we assume that the center of the BD-RIS is located at the origin of the reference Cartesian system, i.e., $\boldsymbol{p _{ris = \left[0, 0 \right]^\mathsf{T}$, and is aligned along the $y$-axis.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Therefore, the position of the $m$-th cell is given by $\boldsymbol{p _{m = [0,y_m]^\mathsf{T}$, where $y_m = m - \frac{M+1 {2}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The BS is positioned at \(\boldsymbol{p _{bs = \left[-d_c, 0 \right]^\mathsf{T}\), where $d_c$ denotes the distance between the BS’s active antenna and the center of the BD-RIS and is on the order of a few wavelengths.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The location of the UE, \(\boldsymbol{p _{ue}\), is unknown and must be estimated.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Accordingly, the distance between the RIS and the UE is expressed as $r = \lVert \boldsymbol{p _{ue - \boldsymbol{p _{ris \rVert$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:System Model , the UE is located at an angular position of $\vartheta = \arcsin{( \left([\boldsymbol{p _{ue ]_1 - [\boldsymbol{p _{ris ]_1) / r\right)}$ relative to the $y$-axis.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: In the proposed system model, two distinct communication channels are defined: $\boldsymbol{g [n] \in \mathbb{C ^{M \times 1}$ for the BS-RIS channel (also known as transmission coefficient vector \cite{10158690, 10515204, 10679332 , 10643881 ) and $\boldsymbol{h [n] \in \mathbb{C ^{1 \times M}$ for the RIS-UE channel.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Here, $n \in [-K,K]$ is an integer representing the $n$-th subcarrier of the employed orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) system, with a total of $N = 2K + 1$ subcarriers.
elements.sent-2411-050: Given the close proximity of the RIS to the BS’s active antenna, the BS-RIS channel requires a distinct model to capture its unique propagation characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-051: The channel coefficient between the active antenna and the $m$-th RIS element at the central frequency is modeled using Rayleigh-Sommerfeld diffraction theory for NF propagation as $\left[\boldsymbol{g [0]\right]_m = \frac{A \cos \chi_m {d_m \left( \frac{1 {2 \pi d_m - \frac{\jmath {\lambda \right) e^{ \jmath 2 \pi d_m / \lambda }$ \cite{10158690, 10515204, 10679332 , 10643881 , where $A = (\lambda/2)^2$ represents the area of each passive element, $d_m$ is the distance between the active antenna and the $m$-th cell, and $\chi_m$ is the angular displacement between the line connecting the active antenna at the BS and the $m$-th cell of the BD-RIS, and the axis perpendicular to the BD-RIS surface.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Accordingly, the BD-RIS channel model for the $n$-th subcarrier is as \cite{10679332 \begin{equation}\label{eq:BS-RIS channel model} \left[\boldsymbol{g}[n]\right]_m = \frac{A \cos \chi_m}{d_m} \left( \frac{1}{2 \pi d_m} - \frac{\jmath}{\lambda} \right) e^{ \jmath 2 \pi d_m \left( \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{n \Delta_f}{c}\right) }, \end{equation} where $\Delta_f = B/N$ denotes the subcarrier spacing with $B$ is the total bandwidth, and $c$ is the speed of light.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The RIS-UE channel depends on the RIS-UE distance $r$ and may be either NF or FF.
elements.sent-2411-054: Notably, the NF model remains valid universally, automatically simplifying to the FF model at far distances.
elements.sent-2411-055: In this paper, however, we treat them separately to allow for analysis of both models.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width = \columnwidth]{Figures/SystemModel.pdf} \caption{System model for the proposed BS-integrated BD-RIS.} \label{fig:System Model}
elements.sent-2411-056: Scenario 1 (NF Channel Model): If $0.62 \sqrt{{D^3 /{\lambda < r < {2D^2 /{\lambda}$, the UE is located in the Fresnel (radiative) NF region of the RIS \cite{10436860, 10017173 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: In this case, the RIS-UE channel is modeled as a line-of-sight (LoS) narrowband channel ($n=0$ and $N=1$), as follows \cite{10135096 :\footnote{To improve readability, we omit the subcarrier index when exclusively discussing Scenario 1 throughout this paper (i.e., $\boldsymbol{h _1[0]$ becomes $\boldsymbol{h _1$). \begin{equation}\label{eq:NF channel} \boldsymbol{h}_1[0] = \boldsymbol{h}_1 = \beta_1 \boldsymbol{a}_1^\mathsf{H}(r,\vartheta), \end{equation} where $\beta_1 = \frac{\lambda {4 \pi r e^{ - \jmath \frac{2 \pi {\lambda r }$ represents the complex channel gain\cite{10436860 .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The term $\boldsymbol{a _1(r, \vartheta) \in \mathbb{C ^{M \times 1}$ denotes the NF array response vector at the RIS, defined as \cite{10135096 : \begin{equation}\label{eq: NF steering vector} \left[ \boldsymbol{a}_1(r,\vartheta) \right]_m = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} e^{ - \jmath \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} (r_m(r,\vartheta) - r) }, \end{equation} where $r_m (r, \vartheta) = \sqrt{r^2 + y_m^2 \delta^2 - 2ry_m\delta \cos(\vartheta)}$ is the distance between the UE and the $m$-th cell.}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq: NF steering vector} \left[ a_1(r,\vartheta) \right]_m = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} e^{ - \jmath \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} (r_m(r,\vartheta) - r) },\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-059: Scenario 2 (FF Channel Model): If $r > {2D^2 /{\lambda}$, the UE is located in the FF region \cite{9145059 .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: In this case, we model the RIS-UE channel as a LoS wideband channel.
elements.sent-2411-061: The channel for the $n$-th subcarrier is expressed as follows \cite{9129075, 9124848 : \begin{equation}\label{eq:FF channel} \boldsymbol{h}_2 [n] = \beta_2 e^{ -\jmath 2 \pi \tau n \Delta_f }\boldsymbol{a}_2^\mathsf{H}(\vartheta), \end{equation} where $\beta_2 = \frac{\lambda {4 \pi r e^{ \jmath \varphi }$ represents the complex channel gain, with $\varphi \sim \mathcal{U (0, 2\pi)$ \cite{9500281 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Here, $\tau = r/c$ is time of arrival (ToA) for RIS-UE channel,\footnote{To simplify analysis, we assume tight synchronization between the BS and UE, leaving asynchronous scenario for the future work.}
elements.sent-2411-063: To facilitate downlink localization, the BS systematically sweeps the environment, transmitting $T_i$ pilot signals to various zones within the area of interest over sequential time slots in the $i$-th scenario.
elements.sent-2411-064: Without loss of generality, we assume a unit transmit pilot symbol.
elements.sent-2411-065: Thus, the received signal at the UE for the $n$-th subcarrier in Scenario $i$ during the $t$-th time slot is as \begin{equation}\label{eq: Signal Model} y_{i,t}[n] = \sqrt{P} \boldsymbol{h}_i[n] \boldsymbol{\Omega}_{i,t} \boldsymbol{g}[n] + w_{i,t}[n], \end{equation} where $P$ is the transmitted power, and $w_{i,t \sim \mathcal{CN (0,\sigma^2)$ represents the additive noise component during the $t$-th time slot in the $i$-th scenario.}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq: Signal Model} y_{i,t}[n] = \sqrt{P} h_i[n] \Omega_{i,t} g[n] + w_{i,t}[n],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-066: Here, $\boldsymbol{\Omega _{i,t \in \mathbb{C ^{M \times M}$ is the RIS phase shift matrix during $t$-th time slot in the $i$-th scenario.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: To ensure that the BD-RIS is power-lossless, its phase shift matrix should satisfy unitary constraint, i.e., $\boldsymbol{\Omega _{i,t ^\mathsf{H \boldsymbol{\Omega _{i,t = \boldsymbol{I _M$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Additionally, the phase shift matrix must be symmetric, i.e., $\boldsymbol{\Omega _{i,t = \boldsymbol{\Omega _{i,t ^\mathsf{T}$, ensuring identical phase adjustments between each pair of passive elements, allowing a single phase shifter per element pair.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Ultimately, for the purpose of the CRLB analysis, we define the noiseless component of the received signal as \begin{equation}\mu_{i,t}[n] = \boldsymbol{h}_i[n] \boldsymbol{\Omega}_{i,t} \boldsymbol{g}[n] = \boldsymbol{h}_i[n] \boldsymbol{\zeta}_{i,t}[n], \end{equation} where $\boldsymbol{\zeta _{i,t [n] = \boldsymbol{\Omega _{i,t \boldsymbol{g [n]$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In each scenario, a pre-defined codebook enables systematic environmental sweeping, with different codewords selected in each time slot to ensure full coverage.
elements.sent-2411-071: Since $T_i$ symbols are required, the codebook must contain $T_i$ codewords, each satisfying the unitary and symmetry constraints outlined above.
elements.sent-2411-072: Takagi’s decomposition, as described in \cite{10187688 , is used to construct this codebook, with detailed steps provided in Algorithm \ref{Alg: Takagi's Decomposition .}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Pre-defined codebook for BD-RIS configuration} \label{Alg: Takagi's Decomposition} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Define $T_i$ uniformly distributed sweeping points/angles for NF/FF: \begin{enumerate} \item[(NF)] Define $T_1$ sweeping points as $\{\boldsymbol{s}_1, \boldsymbol{s}_2, \dots, \boldsymbol{s}_{T_1}\}$ with \{$\boldsymbol{s}_t = [r_t, \vartheta_t]^\mathsf{T} | r_t \in [ \varrho_{min}:\Delta_r:\varrho_{max}]$, $\vartheta_t \in [0: \Delta_{\vartheta} :180^\circ]$\}, where $\varrho_{min} > 0.62\sqrt{\frac{D^3}{\lambda}}$ and $\varrho_{max}<\frac{2 D^2}{\lambda}$. \item[(FF)] Define $T_2$ sweeping angles as $\{\vartheta_1, \vartheta_2, \dots, \vartheta_{T_2}\}$ with $\vartheta_t \in [0: \Delta_{\vartheta} :180^\circ]$. \end{enumerate} \State Compute the transmit steering vector at the transmit terminal of BD-RIS for the $t$-th codeword (associated with the $t$-th time slot): \begin{enumerate} \item[(NF)] Compute toward location $\boldsymbol{s}_t$ using (\ref{eq: NF steering vector}). \item[(FF)] Compute toward direction $\vartheta_t$ using (\ref{eq: FF steering vector}). \end{enumerate} \State Calculate $\boldsymbol{u}_T = \boldsymbol{g}[0] / \lVert \boldsymbol{g}[0] \rVert$; set $\boldsymbol{u}_{R} = \boldsymbol{a}_1(r_t, \vartheta_t)$ for NF or $\boldsymbol{u}_{R} = \boldsymbol{a}_2( \vartheta_t)$ for FF. \State Calculate the symmetric matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$ and decompose it using standard singular value decomposition (SVD) as $\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{u}_{R} \boldsymbol{u}_T^\mathsf{H} + (\boldsymbol{u}_{R} \boldsymbol{u}_T^\mathsf{H})^\mathsf{T} = \boldsymbol{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \boldsymbol{V}^\mathsf{H}$. \State Compute $\boldsymbol{\nu} = \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{U}^\mathsf{H} \boldsymbol{V}^*)$, followed by $\boldsymbol{\phi} = \angle \boldsymbol{\nu} / 2$. \State After computing $\boldsymbol{Q} = \boldsymbol{U} \, \text{diag}(\exp(\jmath \boldsymbol{\phi}))$, Takagi’s decomposition can be expressed as $\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{Q} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \boldsymbol{Q}^\mathsf{T}$; thus, the $t$-th codeword for the $i$-th scenario is obtained as $\boldsymbol{\Omega}_{i,t} = \boldsymbol{Q}\boldsymbol{Q}^\mathsf{T}$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-073: In this section, we calculate the position error bound (PEB), which represents a theoretical lower bound on the achievable accuracy of position estimation.
elements.sent-2411-074: The positional parameters for scenario $i$ are expressed as $\boldsymbol{\xi _{po,i = [\boldsymbol{p _{ue,i ^\mathsf{T , \Re{(\beta_i) , \Im{(\beta_i) ]^\mathsf{T}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: However, before proceeding, we must first derive the CRLB for the channel parameters.
elements.sent-2411-076: In the $i$-th scenario, the Fisher information matrix (FIM), $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{F _{ch,i \in \mathbb{R ^{4 \times 4}$, with respect to the channel parameters set $\boldsymbol{\xi _{ch,i}$ is obtained using the Slepian-Bangs formula \cite{kay1993fundamentals as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq: FIM of channel parameters} \boldsymbol{\mathcal{F}}_{ch,i} = \frac{2P}{\sigma^2} \sum_{t = 1}^{T_i} \sum_{n = -K}^{K} \Re \left\{ \left( \frac{\partial \mu_{i,t}[n]}{\partial \boldsymbol{\xi}_{ch,i}} \right) \left( \frac{\partial \mu_{i,t}[n]}{\partial \boldsymbol{\xi}_{ch,i}} \right) ^\mathsf{H} \right\}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The channel parameter vector for NF and FF scenarios are defined as $\boldsymbol{\xi _{ch,1 = [r, \vartheta, \Re{(\beta_1) , \Im{(\beta_1) ]^\mathsf{T}$ and $\boldsymbol{\xi _{ch,2 = [\tau, \vartheta, \Re{(\beta_2) , \Im{(\beta_2) ]^\mathsf{T}$, respectively.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The mathematical derivations for obtaining $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{F _{ch,i}$ are provided in Appendix \ref{apndx: FIM of channel parameters .}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Consequently, the CRLB for $[\boldsymbol{\xi _{ch,i ]_\ell$ is calculated as: \begin{equation}\eta_\ell = CRLB\left([\boldsymbol{\xi}_{ch,i}]_\ell\right) = \sqrt{[\boldsymbol{\mathcal{F}}_{ch,i}^{-1}]_{\ell,\ell}}. \end{equation}.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Computer Simulation parameters.} \begin{tabular}{c c c c} Parameter Value Parameter Value $\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}$ $[0, 0]^\mathsf{T}$ $T_1$ $500$ $\boldsymbol{p}_{ue,1}$ $[12, 8]^\mathsf{T}$ m $T_2$ $100$ $\boldsymbol{p}_{ue,2}$ $[60, 40]^\mathsf{T}$ m $\Delta_r$ $10$ m $d_c$ $0.5\lambda$ \cite{10515204,10158690} $\Delta_{\vartheta}$ $1.8^{\circ}$ $M$ $101$ $\varrho_{min}$ $5$ m $P$ $20$ dBm $\varrho_{max}$ $45$ m $c$ $3 \times 10^8$ m/s $N_1$ $1$ $f_c = c/\lambda$ $28$ GHz $N_2$ $501$ $\Delta_f$ $120$ KHz $B$ $N_i \Delta_f$ $\delta$ $\lambda/2$ m $\sigma^2$ $-174 + 10\log(B)$ dBm \end{tabular} \label{tab:System parameters}
elements.sent-2411-080: Once $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{F _{ch,i}$ is calculated, the FIM of the positional parameters can be obtained as $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{F _{po,i = \boldsymbol{\mathcal{J _i^\mathsf{T \boldsymbol{\mathcal{F _{ch,i \boldsymbol{\mathcal{J _i$, where $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{J _i \in \mathbb{R ^{4 \times 4}$ is the Jacobian matrix, defined as $\left[ \boldsymbol{\mathcal{J _i \right]_{\ell,s = {\partial \left[ \boldsymbol{\xi _{ch,i \right]_{\ell /{\partial \left[ \boldsymbol{\xi _{po,i \right]_s .$ The mathematical derivation of $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{J _i$ is provided in Appendix \ref{apndx: Jacobian Matrix .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Ultimately, the $PEB$ for Scenario $i$ is calculated as follows: \begin{equation}PEB_i = \sqrt{\trace{\left( [\boldsymbol{\mathcal{F}}_{po,i}^{-1}]_{1:2,1:2} \right)}}. \end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: This section presents the CRLB analysis of the proposed architecture.
elements.sent-2411-083: The assumed values for various parameters are listed in Table \ref{tab:System parameters , unless stated otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-084: For comparison, we evaluate two different benchmarks:.
elements.sent-2411-085: Benchmark 1 (D-RIS): A cell-wise single-connected BD-RIS operating in transmissive mode.
elements.sent-2411-086: It is also called simultaneously transmitting and reflecting RIS (STAR-RIS) \cite{9913356 .}
elements.sent-2411-087: When integrated within a BS, it functions as a single-layer stacked intelligent metasurface \cite{10158690, 10515204, 10679332 , 10643881, 10622963, 10557708, 9913356 .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Due to its single-connected structure and diagonal phase shift matrix, which aligns with the characteristics of traditional D-RIS, we refer to it as D-RIS in this paper for clarity and to highlight its diagonal structure.
elements.sent-2411-089: Benchmark 2 (AAA): The BS equipped with a linear active antenna array (AAA) with $M$ antenna elements.
elements.sent-2411-090: In each benchmark, a pre-defined codebook is constructed for beam-sweeping to facilitate UE localization.
elements.sent-2411-091: For both benchmarks, steps $1-3$ in Algorithm \ref{Alg: Takagi's Decomposition are first performed.}
elements.sent-2411-092: In Benchmark 1, the $t$-th codeword is generated as $\boldsymbol{\Omega _{i,t = \diag\left(e^{-\jmath \arg(\boldsymbol{u _T \odot \boldsymbol{u _R^*)}\right)$ \cite{10187688 , while $\boldsymbol{u _R$ is used to create the $t$-th codeword for Benchmark 2.}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width = \columnwidth]{Figures/BeamPattern.pdf} \caption{Transmit beamforming patterns for BD-RIS and benchmark configurations, calculated using the effective passive beamforming vector $\boldsymbol{\zeta} = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \boldsymbol{g}[0]$.} \label{fig:BeamPattern}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[scale = 0.34]{Figures/CRLB_Analysis_NF_rvrs.pdf} \caption{CRLB and PEB analysis for Scenario 1 (NF): (a) CRLB for $r$ and $\vartheta$ vs. transmitted power, (b) PEB vs. transmitted power, (c) CRLB for $r$ and $\vartheta$ vs. BS-RIS distance, and (d) PEB vs. BS-RIS distance.} \label{fig:NF CRLB analysis}
elements.sent-2411-093: Placing the RIS closer to the BS reduces the multiplicative path loss in the overall BS-UE channel \cite{raeisi2024comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:BeamPattern , D-RIS exhibits reduced beamforming performance as $d_c$ decreases.}
elements.sent-2411-095: This decline occurs because the BS-RIS channel vector $\boldsymbol{g}$ shows greater variations in both magnitude and phase with a shorter $d_c$.
elements.sent-2411-096: Since D-RIS can only adjust the phase, it cannot compensate for these magnitude variations, thereby limiting its beamforming capability \cite{9514409 .}
elements.sent-2411-097: In contrast, the fully-connected BD-RIS can adjust both phase and amplitude of impinging waves, allowing it to sustain high beamforming gain even at close proximity to the BS.
elements.sent-2411-098: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:BeamPattern , this capability enables BD-RIS to achieve beamforming performance comparable to the AAA structure.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Although BD-RIS matches AAA in beamforming gain, AAA is still expected to outperform it due to reduced path loss, as it lacks the cascaded channels effect.
elements.sent-2411-100: Next, we evaluate BD-RIS performance against the benchmarks in terms of PEB and CRLB on channel parameters.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width = \columnwidth]{Figures/CRLB_Analysis_FF_rvrs.pdf} \caption{CRLB and PEB analysis in Scenario 2 (FF): (a) CRLB for $\tau$ and $\vartheta$ vs. transmitted power, (b) PEB vs. transmitted power, (c) CRLB for $\tau$ and $\vartheta$ vs. BS-RIS distance, (d) PEB vs. BS-RIS distance, (e) CRLB for $\tau$ and $\vartheta$ vs. number of subcarriers, and (f) PEB vs. number of subcarriers.} \label{fig:FF CRLB analysis}
elements.subsection-2411-006: CRLB Analysis for the NF Scenario
elements.sent-2411-101: The CRLB analysis for Scenario 1 (NF) is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:NF CRLB analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Figs.~\ref{fig:NF CRLB analysis (a) and (b) present the CRLB for $r$, $\vartheta$, and the PEB across different transmitted power levels, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-103: While a significant performance gap exists between D-RIS and AAA, BD-RIS achieves performance close to AAA due to similar beamforming capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-104: Figs.~\ref{fig:NF CRLB analysis (c) and (d) show the CRLB for channel parameters and PEB as a function of BS-RIS distance ($d_c$), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-105: When the RIS is positioned far from the BS, BD-RIS and D-RIS exhibit similar CRLB performance.
elements.sent-2411-106: This similarity arises from the comparable beamforming gains of both structures at larger $d_c$, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:BeamPattern .}
elements.sent-2411-107: As $d_c$ decreases, BD-RIS performance improves due to reduced multiplicative path loss, with its fully-connected structure maintaining beamforming gain.
elements.sent-2411-108: In contrast, D-RIS initially benefits from reduced $d_c$, but beyond a critical threshold, the loss in beamforming gain outweighs path loss reduction, leading to a substantial CRLB and PEB performance drop.
elements.subsection-2411-007: CRLB Analysis for the FF Scenario
elements.sent-2411-109: Fig.~\ref{fig:FF CRLB analysis presents the CRLB analysis for Scenario 2 (FF), comparing BD-RIS, D-RIS, and AAA across various parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-110: In Fig.~\ref{fig:FF CRLB analysis (a), D-RIS significantly underperforms in $\vartheta$ estimation compared to BD-RIS and AAA, which achieve higher angular resolution due to their sharp beams.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Conversely, D-RIS achieves $\tau$ estimation comparable to BD-RIS and AAA due to its broad beam pattern, which disperses energy over a wide range.
elements.sent-2411-112: This wide dispersion allows the UE to receive pilot signals even when the BS beam is not precisely aimed at it.
elements.sent-2411-113: Notably, $\tau$ estimation primarily relies on the frequency diversity of the wideband channel rather than a sharp, narrow beam, making D-RIS’s broad coverage beneficial for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-114: This advantage is also reflected in the CRLB trends for $\tau$ in Figs.~\ref{fig:FF CRLB analysis (c) and (e).}
elements.sent-2411-115: Despite this advantage in $\tau$, D-RIS’s weaker angular estimation negatively impacts its PEB, while BD-RIS attains performance close to AAA, thanks to its flexibility in beam design, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:FF CRLB analysis (b).}
elements.sent-2411-116: Figs.~\ref{fig:FF CRLB analysis (c) and (d) show the CRLB performance of the channel parameters and the PEB performance as a function of BS-RIS distance ($d_c$), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-117: The trends for $\vartheta$ and PEB closely mirror those in Scenario 1 (NF) (see Figs.~\ref{fig:NF CRLB analysis (c) and (d)).}
elements.sent-2411-118: As $d_c$ decreases, the CRLB on $\tau$ improves for both D-RIS and BD-RIS, driven by reduced multiplicative path loss and enhanced SNR, underscoring SNR’s importance in $\tau$ estimation.
elements.sent-2411-119: Note that, AAA’s performance remains stable, unaffected by $d_c$, due to its independent MISO structure.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/UE_Location_PEB.pdf} \caption{$10\log_{10}(PEB)$ for different UE positions. (a) Scenario 1 (NF); (b) Scenario 2 (FF).} \label{fig: Different UE Positions}
elements.sent-2411-120: Figs.~\ref{fig:FF CRLB analysis (e) and (f) respectively show the CRLB performance for channel parameters and PEB as the number of subcarriers increases.}
elements.sent-2411-121: In Fig.~\ref{fig:FF CRLB analysis (e), $\vartheta$ estimation remains unaffected by the number of subcarriers, as it depends primarily on beamforming gain.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Conversely, $\tau$ estimation benefits from the frequency diversity of OFDM, showing improved CRLB performance across all setups as the number of subcarriers increases.
elements.sent-2411-123: In Fig.~\ref{fig:FF CRLB analysis (f), D-RIS’s PEB performance plateaus after only a few subcarriers, while BD-RIS and AAA continue to improve significantly before reaching their own performance ceilings at much higher subcarrier counts.}
elements.sent-2411-124: This early ceiling for D-RIS is due to its limited beamforming capability and correspondingly poorer $\vartheta$ estimation.
elements.sent-2411-125: In contrast, the lower CRLB on $\vartheta$ for BD-RIS and AAA indicates a greater capacity to leverage frequency diversity across a larger number of subcarriers, thereby enhancing PEB.
elements.subsection-2411-008: PEB Visualization for the Area of Interest
elements.sent-2411-126: Figs.~\ref{fig: Different UE Positions (a) and (b) show the PEB on a logarithmic scale for various UE positions in NF and FF scenarios, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-127: In the NF scenario, the same RIS configuration listed in Table \ref{tab:System parameters is applied.}
elements.sent-2411-128: BD-RIS achieves performance comparable to AAA, while D-RIS provides sub-meter level accuracy only for UEs within a few meters of the RIS.
elements.sent-2411-129: For the FF scenario illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: Different UE Positions (b), we assume $\Delta_{\vartheta = 5^\circ$ (instead of $\Delta_\vartheta = 1.8^\circ$, for better visualization of the beams).}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Similar to NF scenarios, D-RIS shows relatively poor performance, whereas BD-RIS's performance remains close to AAA.
elements.sent-2411-131: Furthermore, Fig.~\ref{fig: Different UE Positions (b) illustrates enhanced PEB along paths where the sweeping beam aligns with the positions of UEs in both BD-RIS and AAA configurations, underscoring the critical role of high-resolution codebooks in achieving precise localization.}
elements.sent-2411-132: While a direct comparison between NF (Fig.~\ref{fig: Different UE Positions (a)) and FF (Fig.~\ref{fig: Different UE Positions (b)) is challenging due to the higher number of samples in FF from the OFDM structure, it is evident that BD-RIS provides a more substantial improvement over D-RIS in NF than in FF.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: In NF scenarios, where beamforming gain is critical for estimating positional parameters $r$ and $\vartheta$, BD-RIS significantly reduces the PEB compared to D-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-134: In contrast, this enhancement is less pronounced in FF, where ToA estimation benefits more from frequency diversity rather than beamforming precision.
elements.sent-2411-135: This paper has proposed a novel BS architecture integrating fully-connected BD-RIS to enable enhanced passive beamforming for efficient localization.
elements.sent-2411-136: This design offers a promising alternative to existing benchmarks, achieving localization accuracy comparable to active analog beamforming with traditional antenna arrays.
elements.sent-2411-137: Through comprehensive CRLB analysis in both NF and FF scenarios, we have demonstrated that BD-RIS offers substantial improvements over traditional D-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-138: Notably, BD-RIS shows comparable performance to AAA, establishing it as a viable solution for high-accuracy positioning in 6G networks and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-139: Our findings underline the potential of BD-RIS to bridge the gap between active and passive localization methods, paving the way for efficient, scalable localization solutions in future networks.. \appendices.
elements.sent-2411-140: Consequently, $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{F _{ch,i}$ is obtained using (\ref{eq: FIM of channel parameters ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Scenario 1: The calculations for Scenario 1 are as follows: \begin{equation}[\frac{\partial \mu_{1,t}}{\partial r}, \frac{\partial \mu_{1,t}}{\partial \vartheta}]^\mathsf{T} = \beta_1 [\boldsymbol{\dot{a}}_{1,r}(r,\vartheta) , \boldsymbol{\dot{a}}_{1,\vartheta}(r,\vartheta)]^\mathsf{H} \boldsymbol{\zeta}_{1,t}[0],\end{equation} \begin{equation}[\frac{\partial \mu_{1,t}}{\partial \Re{(\beta_1)}}, \frac{\partial \mu_{1,t}}{\partial \Im{(\beta_1)}}] = [1, \jmath]\boldsymbol{a}_1^\mathsf{H}(r,\vartheta) \boldsymbol{\zeta}_{1,t}[0],\end{equation} where \begin{equation}[\boldsymbol{\dot{a}}_{1,r}(r,\vartheta),\boldsymbol{\dot{a}}_{1,\vartheta}(r,\vartheta)] = [{\boldsymbol{d}}_r, {\boldsymbol{d}}_{\vartheta}] \odot [\boldsymbol{a}_1(r,\vartheta), \boldsymbol{a}_1(r,\vartheta)],\end{equation} with \begin{equation}[{\boldsymbol{d}}_r]_m = - \jmath \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \left( \frac{r - y_m \delta \cos{\vartheta}}{\sqrt{r^2 + y_m^2 \delta^2 - 2 r y_m \delta \cos{\vartheta}}} - 1 \right),\end{equation} \begin{equation}[{\boldsymbol{d}}_{\vartheta}]_m = - \jmath \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \frac{r y_m \delta \sin{\vartheta}}{\sqrt{r^2 + y_m^2 \delta^2 - 2 r y_m \delta \cos{\vartheta}}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-142: Scenario 2: The calculations in this case are as follows: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \mu_{2,t} [n]}{\partial \tau} = - \jmath 2 \pi n \Delta_f \beta_2 e^{-\jmath 2 \pi \tau n \Delta_f } \boldsymbol{a}_2^\mathsf{H}(\vartheta) \boldsymbol{\zeta}_{2,t}[n],\end{equation} \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \mu_{2,t} [n]}{\partial \vartheta} = \beta_2 e^{ -\jmath 2 \pi \tau n \Delta_f } \boldsymbol{\dot{a}}_{2,\vartheta}^\mathsf{H}(\vartheta) \boldsymbol{\zeta}_{2,t}[n],\end{equation} \begin{equation}[\frac{\partial \mu_{2,t} [n]}{\partial \Re{(\beta_2)}}, \frac{\partial \mu_{2,t} [n]}{\partial \Im{(\beta_2)}}] = [1,\jmath] e^{-\jmath 2 \pi \tau n \Delta_f } \boldsymbol{a}_2^\mathsf{H}(\vartheta) \boldsymbol{\zeta}_{2,t}[n],\end{equation} where $\boldsymbol{\dot{a _{2,\vartheta (\vartheta) = -\jmath 2 \pi \frac{\delta {\lambda \cos{\vartheta \diag(0,\dots,M-1) \boldsymbol{a _2(\vartheta)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: Scenario 1: The derivatives are as follows: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial r}{\partial [\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_1} = \frac{[\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_1 - [\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}]_1}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{ue} - \boldsymbol{p}_{ris} \rVert}, \frac{\partial r}{\partial [\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_2} = \frac{[\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_2 - [\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}]_2}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{ue} - \boldsymbol{p}_{ris} \rVert}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \vartheta}{\partial [\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_1} = - \frac{[\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_2 - [\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}]_2}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{ue} - \boldsymbol{p}_{ris} \rVert ^2}, \frac{\partial \vartheta}{\partial [\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_2} = \frac{[\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_1 - [\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}]_1}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{ue} - \boldsymbol{p}_{ris} \rVert ^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial r}{\partial [p_{ue}]_1} = \frac{[p_{ue}]_1 - [p_{ris}]_1}{\lVert p_{ue} - p_{ris} \rVert}, \frac{\partial r}{\partial [p_{ue}]_2} = \frac{[p_{ue}]_2 - [p_{ris}]_2}{\lVert p_{ue} - p_{ris} \rVert}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-144: Scenario 2: The derivatives are as follows: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \tau}{\partial [\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_1} = \frac{[\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_1 - [\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}]_1}{c \lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{ue} - \boldsymbol{p}_{ris} \rVert}, \frac{\partial \tau}{\partial [\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_2} = \frac{[\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_2 - [\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}]_2}{c \lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{ue} - \boldsymbol{p}_{ris} \rVert},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \vartheta}{\partial [\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_1} = - \frac{[\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_2 - [\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}]_2}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{ue} - \boldsymbol{p}_{ris} \rVert ^2}, \frac{\partial \vartheta}{\partial [\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_2} = \frac{[\boldsymbol{p}_{ue}]_1 - [\boldsymbol{p}_{ris}]_1}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{ue} - \boldsymbol{p}_{ris} \rVert ^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-145: In both scenarios, it is evident that $\frac{\partial \Re{(\beta_i) {\partial \Re{(\beta_i) = \frac{\partial \Im{(\beta_i) {\partial \Im{(\beta_i) = 1.$ All other entries in the Jacobian matrix \(\boldsymbol{\mathcal{J _i\) are zero.}}}}}}}}}}
